prior.title: Louisiana State Freight Plan 2024 and Commodity Flow Dashboard
prior.summary: Louisiana Department of Transportation and Development (LADOTD) provides a comprehensive state freight plan and commodity flow analysis tool designed to support economic growth through efficient multimodal goods movement. The plan offers strategic guidance on freight infrastructure, supply chain resilience, and equitable transportation solutions with detailed commodity-level data by parish and region.
prior.full_brief:
# Louisiana State Freight Plan 2024 — Operations Profile

---

## # 1. Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief synthesises publicly available material from the Louisiana Department of Transportation and Development (LADOTD) 2024 State Freight Plan (SFP) cycle, corroborating LADOTD fact sheets on ports/waterways and highway freight, and the FHWA State Freight Planning Toolkit. It is an **Operations Profile** anchored to a workspace operating as a freight forwarder with primary air, secondary road, and tertiary ocean modes across Americas and international trade lanes, handling cargo verticals including live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian freight.

**Conventions:** Inline citations follow each factual claim. Items requiring legal review are labeled *Legal Confirmation Required:*. Analytical statements open with a labeled token. The workspace is never named. Source URLs are copied verbatim from the source blocks and corroborator list provided; no invented URLs appear.

**Coverage honest note:** The three source blocks retrievable for this generation contain limited machine-readable detail. The primary landing page (https://latransportationplan.la.gov/freight-mobility-plan/) rendered navigational and structural content only. The Federal Register URL was access-blocked. The FHWA toolkit page rendered a state-list index only. The three named corroborating PDFs (Full Plan PDF, Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet, Highway Freight Fact Sheet) were listed as leads but their full text was not returned in the source content blocks. Consequently, granular data (commodity volumes, specific port throughput figures, exact lane cost benchmarks) that would normally populate Sections 1–3 in full is identified as explicit gaps below. Sections are populated only where verbatim source spans are available or where labeled inference from the structural content is appropriate.

---

## # 2. What's Moving and What Triggered It

**MONITORING**

Louisiana's LADOTD has published the 2024 State Freight Plan (SFP), the state's current multimodal freight strategy document. *Source: Louisiana State Freight Plan landing page, LADOTD/HNTB, 2024. https://latransportationplan.la.gov/freight-mobility-plan/

The plan's stated purpose is to provide "a blueprint for facilitating continued economic growth through a comprehensive, multimodal strategy for addressing freight transportation needs and moving goods efficiently and safely throughout the state." *Source: Louisiana State Freight Plan landing page, LADOTD/HNTB, 2024. https://latransportationplan.la.gov/freight-mobility-plan/

Specifically, the plan states it will: "Meet federal guidelines and support future planning, policies and investment in Louisiana; Support business case for freight system investments; Promote safe, efficient, and resilient goods mobility and supply chain support in Louisiana; Develop data to support local and regional mobility planning; and Reflect input from private sector freight stakeholders." *Source: Louisiana State Freight Plan landing page, LADOTD/HNTB, 2024. https://latransportationplan.la.gov/freight-mobility-plan/

The FHWA monitors and approves all State Freight Plan updates to ensure compliance with current federal surface transportation laws. *Source: FHWA State Freight Planning Toolkit — All Plans, FHWA Office of Freight Management and Operations, current. https://ops.fhwa.dot.gov/freight/fpcb/toolkit/allplans.aspx

*Specific effective date of the 2024 SFP's adoption by FHWA not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-11.*

*Analytical inference:* Federal approval of a state freight plan under the current surface transportation framework (IIJA/Bipartisan Infrastructure Law) is a prerequisite for Louisiana accessing NHFP (National Highway Freight Program) formula funding, which means the 2024 SFP's adoption directly gates capital investment availability on corridors the workspace uses for drayage and road-secondary freight.

---

## # 3. Operational Cost Baseline for the Region

*Specific industrial electricity rates for Louisiana freight operations not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-11.*

*Specific diesel fuel prices for Louisiana not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-11.*

*Specific port handling tariff rates for Louisiana ports not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-11.*

*Specific drayage rates for Louisiana corridors not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-11.*

*Specific labor rates for freight handling in Louisiana not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-11.*

**Research gap:** The corroborating PDFs (Full Plan PDF, Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet, Highway Freight Fact Sheet) are the authoritative sources for these figures. These documents were identified as leads but their full machine-readable text was not available in the source content blocks for this generation. The Ports & Waterways Fact Sheet (https://latransportationplan.la.gov/wp-content/uploads/DOTD_Factsheet_Ports-Waterways_2025-03-21.pdf) and Highway Freight Fact Sheet (https://latransportationplan.la.gov/wp-content/uploads/DOTD_Factsheet_Highway-Freight_2024.pdf) are the recommended primary retrieval targets for a follow-on generation to populate this section.

*Analytical inference:* Louisiana's position as a major Gulf Coast freight hub — anchored by the Port of New Orleans, Port of South Louisiana, and Port of Baton Rouge — creates cost and capacity dynamics that differ materially from inland US freight corridors; workspaces routing ocean-tertiary cargo through Gulf ports face both competitive tariff advantages and infrastructure congestion risk depending on commodity flow season.

---

## # 4. Feasibility of Specific Operational Choices

*Specific restrictions or permissions on on-site solar, BESS, or specific equipment at Louisiana freight facilities not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-11.*

*Analytical inference:* The SFP's stated goal to "promote safe, efficient, and resilient goods mobility and supply chain support in Louisiana" suggests the plan addresses infrastructure resilience — a relevant consideration for workspaces handling high-value or time-sensitive cargo verticals (live events, film and TV, fine art) where single-point-of-failure port or road access creates outsized risk. However, specific resilience investments or facility-level operational restrictions cannot be confirmed without the full plan text.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether specific Louisiana state or parish-level permits govern the installation of temporary power infrastructure at freight staging facilities used for live events or film and TV cargo requires review of applicable Louisiana administrative code, which is outside the scope of this brief.

---

## # 5. Cost Comparison Against Alternatives

*Specific breakeven, payback, or cost-comparison data between road and ocean modes in Louisiana not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-11.*

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating air-primary with road-secondary, Louisiana's extensive inland waterway network (a key feature of the state freight system given the Mississippi River and its tributaries) represents an ocean-tertiary alternative for non-time-sensitive freight that may offer cost offsets versus road drayage on bulk or project cargo — but no cost figures can be confirmed from the source blocks available.

---

## # 6. Cross-Regional Strategic Implications

The SFP explicitly addresses multimodal strategy at the state level and is designed to "Develop data to support local and regional mobility planning." *Source: Louisiana State Freight Plan landing page, LADOTD/HNTB, 2024. https://latransportationplan.la.gov/freight-mobility-plan/

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces with Americas trade lane exposure, Louisiana's freight plan feeds directly into corridor-level investment decisions on I-10, I-20, I-49, and river port access — corridors that connect Gulf import/export gateways to inland distribution points serving the Southeast, Midwest, and Texas markets. Capital investment prioritised in the SFP over its planning horizon would affect road-secondary drayage reliability and cost on these lanes.

*Analytical inference:* The SFP's alignment with federal guidelines under the IIJA framework means Louisiana investment decisions are also tied to national freight network designations. Workspaces routing cargo through Louisiana should monitor whether specific corridors are designated or re-designated as Primary Highway Freight System (PHFS) routes, as this affects truck size/weight access and future infrastructure investment priority.

*Analytical inference:* For humanitarian cargo vertical operations, Louisiana's Gulf ports and the state's role as a primary entry point for Caribbean and Latin American freight make SFP corridor investment decisions directly relevant to last-mile access and emergency surge capacity planning.

---

## # 7. Competitive Positioning in the Region

*Specific competitor operational activity in Louisiana not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-11.*

*No content for this section as of 2026-06-11: No named competitor data was present in the source content blocks or corroborating material returned for this generation.*

---

## # 8. Client Conversation Talking Points

Based on what is confirmed in the source blocks:

**What the workspace can credibly say:**
- Louisiana has published a new 2024 State Freight Plan setting out the state's multimodal freight investment strategy and priorities, aligned with federal IIJA guidelines.
- The plan is subject to FHWA monitoring and approval, ensuring it tracks with national freight network investment frameworks.
- The plan explicitly incorporates private sector freight stakeholder input, meaning shipper and forwarder priorities are part of the formal record.

**What to avoid claiming:**
- Do not quote specific commodity volumes, port throughput rankings, or infrastructure investment dollar figures until the full PDF has been retrieved and span-grounded; those figures require the plan document itself.
- Do not characterise the plan's infrastructure commitments as funded or scheduled without confirming against specific project listings in the plan.

**Questions to pose to clients with Louisiana supply chain exposure:**
- Has your carrier or terminal assessed whether your freight corridors are designated on Louisiana's prioritised freight network under the 2024 plan?
- For time-sensitive verticals (live events, fine art, film), have you mapped single-point-of-failure risk on Louisiana road and port access against the plan's resilience commitments?

---

## # 9. Pending Changes That Shift the Calculus

**MONITORING — re-check window: Q3 2026**

Federal Register guidance on multimodal state freight plans was published 24 February 2026, referencing document number 2026-03648. *Source: Federal Register document 2026-03648, FHWA/USDOT, 24 February 2026. https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/02/24/2026-03648/guidance-on-multimodal-state-freight-plans-and-state-freight-advisory-committees

*Specific content of that guidance not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-11:* The Federal Register URL was access-blocked during this generation. The document title indicates it concerns guidance on multimodal state freight plans and state freight advisory committees — which directly governs how Louisiana's 2024 SFP must be structured and updated.

*Analytical inference:* If the February 2026 federal guidance imposes new requirements on state freight plans (for example, updated data requirements, climate resilience provisions, or supply chain security provisions), Louisiana may need to update or supplement its 2024 SFP to remain FHWA-compliant, which could trigger a new public comment cycle and revised investment priorities. Workspaces with Louisiana corridor dependency should monitor the content of this federal guidance.

*Analytical inference:* The timing of this federal guidance (February 2026, one-to-two years after Louisiana's 2024 plan publication) creates a potential misalignment window during which the state plan may pre-date and not yet reflect updated federal requirements. Re-check recommended: Q3 2026, by which time LADOTD response to the federal guidance is likely to be visible in public planning documents.

**No additional pending changes confirmed from available source content.**

---
